data.title: Copa Holdings, S.A.: A Solid Value Opportunity With A Superior Yield
data.description:
Copa Holdings, S.A. (NYSE:CPA) is an airline company operating from its central hub in Panama City. The company boasts an expansive flight network throughout the Americas, and, with the success of its low-cost business model, has been seeing its number of passengers climb by double digits. Its operational success also includes driving down its cost per average seat miles, highlighting strong execution on the cost control side. In addition to a very attractive dividend yield of 6.6%, the stock also boasts the highest EBITDA growth among its peers at the lowest multiples. I rate the stock as a buy.

Overview

Copa Holdings, S.A. is a Panama-based holding company that owns Copa Airlines, a leading Latin American passenger airline and cargo carrier. The company has a market cap exceeding $4 billion, and with a low-cost business model, is among the leading players in the region.

As of the year-end in 2023, the company operates a fleet of 106 aircraft of various Boeing models. With its Panama base of operations acting as the hub to its expansive network, Copa Airlines connects passengers to and from various locations in South, Central, and North America, as well as the Caribbean islands.

In its central positioning in Panama, which lies in the crossroads of the Americas, Copa Air has the strategic advantage of an extremely valuable location. This gives it access to some of the most high economic growth and densely populated regions in the Western hemisphere. As a result, the company is well-positioned in the industry to capitalize on-demand strength and sustained growth. It has been active in increasing its fleet size to enhance passenger capacity, whilst simultaneously working on expanding its network to include more destinations.

As we shall see below, the company continues to make solid progress on the operational front, with a strong focus on cost reduction, in order to enhance overall profitability.

Q2 Assessment

Operationally speaking, Q2 was among the best quarters the company experienced, especially in terms of cost efficiency, which makes the company highly attractive compared to its peers in the airlines industry.

Despite Q2 being among the seasonally weakest quarters for the company, the company saw its passenger traffic climb by 10.6%, compared to Q2 in 2023, indicating booming demand for its services. Compared to Q2 last year, which saw 2.98 million passengers, this time the company served 3.30 million passengers.

Not only that, but it also saw its capacity increase by 9.7%, to $7.4 billion in available seat miles. With the increase in both passenger traffic and capacity, Copa managed to drive up its load factor by 0.7%, showing efficient management of capacity based on climbing demand.

While all of the metrics mentioned above broadly paint a promising picture of progress, I am especially drawn to the strong execution on the cost side. In terms of unit costs excluding fuel, or the Cost per Average Seat Mile (CASM), Copa saw a 5.8% YoY decline owing to reduced sales and distribution costs, as well as lower aircraft maintenance costs.

The improvement on the sales and distribution side comes as more customers are booking flights directly through the airlines, and by-passing third-party channels like travel agents. There has also been more utilization of the NDC travel agency channel, which typically acts as a lower-cost platform. Moreover, on the maintenance side, the company enjoyed a positive adjustment in leased aircraft return provisions. Together, each of these factors have led to a very attractive cost structure in the current quarter.

Overall, although the company saw its EBITDA drop by 11.3%, during the quarter to $239 million, its operational metrics are all very positive. Moreover, the company's operating margin of 19.47% is the second highest Q2 margin recorded in the company's history.

I must also point out that the company is keen on returning value to its shareholders by means of dividend payments. The company follows a consistent policy of paying out 40% of the prior year's adjusted earnings to shareholders. Currently, CPA boasts a very attractive dividend yield (ttm) of 6.6%. This is the highest yield on any airline stock, by a significant margin:

CPA was impacted by the Covid hit the entire aviation industry took, and as a result, stopped dividend payments between 2021 and 2023. Now that the industry is back on its feet, and the company is generating operational cash flows of nearly over $1 billion annually, it has reinstated its dividend policy, rewarding its shareholders with its stellar gains.

Valuation

I strongly believe that CPA is undervalued, and I have the strong arguments for this case. Firstly, compared to its peers, the company boasts the highest forward-looking EBITDA growth rate, at the lowest non-GAAP PE (FY1) ratio.

To demonstrate this, I have selected five additional peers for the company from the passenger airline industry, each of which have a market cap ranging from $1 billion to $8 billion. These companies are shown below:

For each of the companies, I have decided to plot the EBITDA growth rate (FWD) against the FY1 Non-GAAP PE ratios on a scatter chart. Note that all the data I have used has been collected from the growth and valuation pages of each respective company from Seeking Alpha. The relationship between these metrics is shown below:

I have added a trend line to see the general relationship between future growth and PE ratio. Based on this, it is pretty clear that CPA offers the highest growth, at the lowest price, making it the most valuable play in the peer group.

The second reason, is the company's forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 4.52. Not only is this among the lowest in the company's peer group, it is also significantly below CPA's own historical average of 20.03. This means the company's forward EV/EBITDA is presently 77.42% lower than the average it has been known to trade at in the last 5 years. To me, this seriously spells an undervaluation, which is why I rate the stock as a buy.

Risk

While I find CPA's profile to be extremely promising, with its operational strides and its value proposition, of course, my thesis isn't without risk. However, as opposed to one major risk, there is a range of minor risks, which, if were to all play out, could impact the company's operational performance. Of these, I will mention the two that I find most concerning.

Firstly, due to the political volatility in Venezuela, there has been on and off travel disruption to and from the country, which has directly been impacting Copa Airlines' operations there. This is particularly impactful because, as a network carrier, this does not only obstruct the Panama to Venezuela flight routes, but also wider connecting networks. So while flights to Venezuela reflect 2% of CPA's total capacity, the revenue impact is more than double due to connecting flights. Due to these disruptions, the company already slightly trimmed its guidance on unit revenues, for the full year 2024. However, despite already accounting for the situation, further escalation in the country could bode negatively for CPA, and may lead to an even more prolonged period of flight restrictions and reduced demand of flights to and from the region.

Additionally, weakening currencies in the Central and South American regions, particularly in Brazil, which represents a major market for the company, adds a further layer of uncertainty to deal with. This devaluation creates pressure on point-of-sale demand, where local ticket sales in Brazil may weaken as fares become relatively more expensive in the local currency. In such a case, customers may opt for low-cost carriers, such as the Azul Brazilian Airlines.

Despite these risks being somewhat concerning, I am reassured by the fact that they have been acknowledged by the company's management, and factored into the guidance for the year. As a result, I don't see the company being hit by an adverse surprise situation.

Takeaway

Copa Airlines is a well-positioned business with a strategic location that allows it to capitalize on strong demand trends coming from the high population and high economic growth regions in the Americas. The company has been executing well on its low-cost model, bringing down its cost per average seat miles significantly, all while managing surging capacity and a jump in passenger numbers. Its dividend yield of 6.6% is the highest in the airlines industry, and its EBITDA growth is the most attractive. With all these strengths, the stock still has the lowest non-GAAP PE ratio (FY1). I think CPA is very valuable, and therefore, I rate the stock as a buy.

data.title: Microsoft: It's Time To Look Elsewhere (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) with a “buy” rating on April 29th 2024 in “Microsoft: Vulnerable to Downside Corrections”, the stock was in the process of falling from the April 11th highs of $429.37 (in a move that resulted in losses of -9.26%). Overall, the thesis of my bullish article was that these were the types of declines that tend to produce in the best buying opportunities for this stock and that those bearish moves represented a new opportunity to add to long positions:

Following my publication, MSFT share prices quickly reversed higher and generated gains of 16.71% over the next nine weeks, showing that well-structured dip buying strategies can be an excellent approach to maximizing gains:

Since then, Microsoft has released quarterly results for the fiscal fourth-quarter period that surpassed consensus estimates for both earnings and revenue. However, weaknesses within the Azure cloud segment were enough to worry investors, and the stock quickly gave back all of its prior gains while falling to near-term lows of $385.60 on August 5th. Specifically, EPS figures came in at $2.95 (slightly beating estimates calling for $2.93 per share) and revenues came in at $64.73 billion (surpassing estimates calling for $64.39 billion). Additionally, the quarter’s net income figure of $22 billion indicates annualized growth rates of 10% while gross margin figures posted annualized gains of 14%:

Overall, revenue growth came in at 15% and Microsoft’s guidance figures suggest current quarter revenues are likely to post within a $63.8-64.8 billion range. At the midpoint, this would imply revenue growth of just under 14% for the period. However, given the market’s most recent negative reaction to earnings, it seems these results (if realized) might not be enough to generate significant share price rallies near-term.

In my view, part of the reason for this lies in the fact that Microsoft’s forward price-earnings valuation (at 31.88x) suggests that there might be better options right now within the MAG-7 complex. To be fair, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is currently trading with an extremely high forward price-earnings valuation of 95.17x while Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) is also relatively elevated levels of 48.56x. Apple, Inc. (AAPL) is a bit more in-line with Microsoft’s current valuation (at 31.26x). Two stock choices that are looking much more well-positioned at the moment can be found with Meta Platforms (META) at 27.61x and Alphabet, Inc. (GOOG) at just 21.75x.

An even worse picture starts to emerge if we look at these same companies through the lens of the forward price-sales metric, where only Nvidia (at 26.95x) is trading with a higher ratio relative to Microsoft (at 11.16x). Of course, this means that five of these seven stocks are currently trading at lower valuations - and some of these selections are significantly cheaper. META (at 9.23x) and AAPL (at 8.38x) are somewhat close using this specific valuation metric. However, TSLA (at 6.99x), GOOG (at 5.89x) and AMZN (at just 3.1x) are looking much more attractive right now.

Last, I’d like to look at the relative price-book ratio that is seen throughout this mega-cap cohort. Once again, Microsoft is trading within the upper half of this group (at 11.6x) while Nvidia (at 58.19x) and Apple (at 52.74x) are obviously much more expensive. However, once again, we are seeing several MAG-7 options that are trading at much lower valuations at the moment. Specifically, we can point to Tesla (at 10.53x), Amazon (at 8.33x), Meta Platforms (at 9.52x) and Alphabet (at just 6.81x) as potentially preferable options. When we are looking at these comparative valuation ratios, one attractive MAG-7 stock that stands out to me in particular is Alphabet - and this is why I recently upgraded my GOOG rating to a “strong buy” in my article “Google: Buy Before Earnings” on October 14th.

Given the general weakness that we have seen in Microsoft’s most recent post-earnings share price performances, I am a bit reluctant to recommend adding to long positions at current levels. Additionally, we are starting to see the formation of what might actually be a head and shoulders pattern on the daily time frames. If this type of price pattern became apparent on a shorter-term time frame, the overall significant of the formation might be reduced dramatically. However, the daily chart is arguably the most commonly watched time frame in the entire market, so it stands to reason that many other investors might be seeing this bearish pattern as well. Thus, if we combine this price activity with recent market tendencies to sell shares of MSFT following earnings and the underwhelming forward valuation metrics that characterize Microsoft in relation to its MAG-7 peer group, it becomes easier to see why I am currently downgrading my outlook for this stock to a “hold” rating.

In order for me to change this viewpoint, I would need to see markets respond favorably to Microsoft’s next earnings release and engage in enough buying activity to send share prices through important resistance levels that are now found at the September 19th, 2024 highs (of $441.50). If this occurs, my next upside price target for this stock would rest at the stock's all-time highs of $468.35 (which was recorded during the July 5th, 2024 trading session). Unfortunately, guidance figures for the fiscal first quarter look a bit unimpressive at the moment, with the possible exception of the company’s expected figures for the Azure Cloud segment (with annualized growth estimates currently found between 28-29% for the period). However, growth rates for this segment were previously seen at similar levels and this failed to elicit a bullish response from the market (and the stock ultimately declined in the periods that followed).

For these reasons, I think that we will need to see upside surprises within this segment that are above 30% in order to see MSFT share prices break the aforementioned resistance levels. Conversely, another post-earnings selloff would actually cause me to further reduce my rating outlook for this stock (to a “sell” rating) in the event that share prices breach key support zones currently found at $385.58 (which is the historical low from August 5th, 2024). In this case, we would be seeing a downside break in the “neckline” of the head and shoulders pattern that I outlined previously, and this is the type of event that could cause share price momentum to accelerate in the bearish direction. Of course, this would be the most bearish extent of my current expectations for this stock, but this is the type of outcome that I would need to see before considering selling my MSFT position. Overall, I am a bit disheartened by the fact that other stocks within the MAG-7 cohort are trading with more attractive forward valuations right now, and this is the sentiment that really forms the basis for my decision to downgrade Microsoft to my current “hold” rating.

data.title: Wesdome Gold Mines: Turnaround Thesis Remains Intact
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted with a C$ in front of the figure. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day.

Q2 Production & Sales

Wesdome Gold Mines Ltd. (“Wesdome”)(TSX:WDO:CA) (OTCQX:WDOFF) released its Q2-24 results in mid-August, reporting record quarterly production of ~44,000 ounces of gold, representing a 42% increase in the year-ago period. The sharp increase in production can be attributed to the successful ramp of up Kiena and mining of high-grade Kiena Deep ore that began in mid-April, providing a significant boost to Kiena’s quarterly performance. In fact, production at its new Kiena Mine more than tripled on a year-over-year basis to ~24,800 ounces, edging out its legacy Eagle River Mine. Not surprisingly, this leverage on fixed costs with much higher production and sales translated to far better cost performance, with Kiena’s all-in sustaining costs [AISC] improving from $2,052/oz to $1,123/oz.

Digging into the Q2-24 results a little closer, Kiena processed ~57,700 tonne at 13.5 G/T of gold, a ~170% increase from 5.0 G/T of gold in the year-ago period. As noted, the higher grades benefited from a much higher proportion of ultra high-grade Kiena Deep ore vs. a greater reliance on the lower grade Martin, S50 and VC zones as the company worked to access the 129 Level. The good news is that Kiena is now benefiting from these significantly higher grades, and the company noted that mined grades at Kiena exceeded its expectations with a hockey-stick like increase in grades vs. the gradual ramp up previously anticipated. In fact, Wesdome’s former CEO stated that “grades are comparing favorably to our short-term model” and that productivity was in line with expectations.

Just as importantly as mined grades, recoveries improved further year-over-year with the benefit of high-grade ore, with Kiena’s recovery rates coming in at 99%, confirming the elevated recoveries expected in the 2021 PFS (98.5%) from the Kiena Deep zones. It’s also worth noting that the significantly improved AISC (Q2-24: $1,123/oz) would have been even better and closer to ~$1,050/oz if not for fewer ounces sold than produced. Finally, Wesdome looks positioned to deliver on Kiena guidance of 85,000 ounces, with ~33,200 ounces produced year-to-date but a back-end-weighed H2-24 with the benefit of full access to Kiena Deep ore vs. just three months of mining in H1-24.

The latter would be a welcome development, as would be delivering on its annual guidance midpoint of 170,000 ounces. This is because Wesdome is currently sporting the worst three-year trailing track record of delivery on guidance vs. its midpoint, with an average 23.5% miss vs. guidance. In fairness, Wesdome's new CEO seems to be doing a better job managing expectations since joining Wesdome at the beginning of Q3-23, with Wesdome tracking well to beat its 2024 guidance (~45.5% based on year-to-date production of ~77,400 ounces) with 55%+ expected in H2-24.

Moving over to the company's Eagle River Mine, production came in at ~19,300 ounces, a 15% decline from the year-ago period. Lower production was related to lower throughput (~52,600 tonnes) and lower grades, offset by slightly higher grades (11.8 G/T of gold) and marginally lower recoveries in the period. Wesdome noted that the dip in throughput was partially related to a maintenance shut in the last week of Q2. Unfortunately, the combination of fewer ounces sold, and higher sustaining capital resulted in a spike in AISC to $1,860/oz, up 26% year-over-year, and costs will remain well above the industry average in H2-24. Wesdome stated the following in relation to the asset:

"Optimization remains a key priority at Eagle River, where costs have escalated over the last number of years. We have identified initiatives that are at various stages of execution and anticipate that results will yield value over the next year. We're in the process of examining productivity drivers, cost inputs, and our maintenance practices for value."

- Wesdome Mines Q2-24 Conference Call.

As for these initiatives, Wesdome noted that it should be able to share more by the end of this year, with one being the potential to fill the 1,200 TPD of permitted mill capacity to better leverage fixed costs. On a positive note, while this will be a lower production and higher-cost year at Eagle River (guidance midpoint: 85,000 ounces at $1,625/oz AISC), Eagle River is gearing up for a better 2025 which should result in an improved cost profile if its optimization work is successful. Plus, while the increase in costs is disappointing, it's at least being more than offset by the record gold price which looks like it could average $2,500/oz in H2-24.

To summarize, Q2-24 was a solid quarter overall, with Kiena Deep ore helping to offset a softer quarter for production and sales at Eagle River. Meanwhile, the heavy lifting is done at Kiena and grades are reconciling well to date, a positive sign relative to the weaker ramp-ups we've seen at other high-grade underground mines in Canada over the past few years (Madsen — Ontario, Premier — British Columbia). Let's take a look at costs and margins:

Costs & Margins

Looking at Wesdome's Q2-24 cost performance, AISC came in at $1,445/oz, a material improvement from the $1,666/oz reported in the year-ago period. This was despite higher sustaining capital year-over-year with unit costs benefiting from record production and far lower costs at Kiena which is now performing closer to design with it milling nearly half an ounce per tonne ore. The company also benefited from a slightly weaker Canadian Dollar in Q2-24. However, with two of the largest gold producers in Canada ramping up production near its operations (Magino, Island Gold, Kirkland Lake Camp, Canadian Malartic), it's possible Wesdome and other smaller Canadian miners could see some pressure on labor going forward.

Looking at Wesdome's margins, it saw a material increase in margins to $888/oz vs. $300/oz in the year-ago period. This translated to a 2200+ basis point increase in AISC margins to 38.1%, benefiting from lapping easy year-over-year comps given that Kiena was still processing lower grades as it developed towards the 129 Level. Wesdome also benefited from a record average realized gold price of $2,333/oz. We will see further margin expansion with a full quarter of production from Kiena Deep and what should be an average realized gold price of $2,450/oz or better in Q3-24. Notably, the Q3-24 margin outlook will be helped by lapping a weak quarter for gold in Q3-23, with AISC margins set to soar as Wesdome laps another quarter of easy comps (Q3-23 AISC margins: 13.3%).

Given the significant increase in production and margins in Q2-24, Wesdome's financial results also improved materially. In fact, revenue soared 51% to C$127.8 million, operating cash flow more than tripled to ~C$57 million, and the company was able to fully repay its revolving credit facility, ending the quarter with over C$50 million in cash. Meanwhile, free cash flow increased to ~C$28 million despite higher capex, and Wesdome is on track to generate well over C$100 million in free cash flow this year.

This is a significant improvement from 2023 levels with Wesdome sporting higher production, a more respectable profile and a clean balance sheet to support its planned organic growth. And while the share price has more than reflected this after doubling off its lows, the recent operational execution has improved the investment thesis vs. 18 months ago. Then, there were worries about additional share issuance on the ATM after a slower than planned ramp-up at Kiena.

Recent Developments

As for recent developments, Wesdome noted that it is drilling to test zones that could be mined with its ongoing Presqu'ile ramp development. Presqu'ile could provide a 250+ TPD lift to throughput by late 2025 to take advantage of significant excess capacity at the Kiena Mill, potentially boosting Kiena production by 20,000+ ounces on an annualized basis. Wesdome shared that the Presqu'ile ramp was sitting near 450 meters of a planned 2.2 kilometers, while the 33 Level drift allows for more effective exploration east of Kiena.

It's important to note even with additional ore being sent from Presqu'ile in 2026 that its new Kiena Mine will be operating at less than half its permitted capacity currently of ~2,000 TPD. Hence, there is room to grow this operation even further longer-term and above its expected average annual production profile of ~105,000 ounces with the addition of the Presqu'ile Zone.

As for its Eagle River Mine, Wesdome shared that it made a new discovery closer to the surface with the 6 Central Zone. It was expanded down-plunge to the east and recently reported highlight intercepts of 2.8 meters at 71.7 G/T of gold and 2.6 meters at 93.7 G/T of gold. This zone is near existing infrastructure and has been delineated over a 180-meter plunge and ~150 meters along strike to date. That said, while the highlight intercepts from this new zone are very impressive, we can see that they are quite significant outliers to date. In fact, the average intercept into this zone comes in closer to 1.9 meters at 6.3 G/T of gold when removing the best hole and placing caps on the highest-grade hits. This is ~1.9 meters at 8.5 G/T of gold on average across all intercepts.

Obviously, these average grades are nothing to sneeze at and still quite impressive. There is the potential for high single-digit grades after factoring in mining dilution, but they don't match up to Eagle River's ~400,000 ounce reserve base at 17.4 G/T of gold to date. Elsewhere at Eagle River, Wesdome continues to have success drilling the Falcon 311 one which was extended another 250 meters down-plunge with a highlight intercept of 3.5 meters at 33.0 G/T of gold (31.8 G/T of gold capped).

As for other recent developments, we've seen a significant shake-up in management, which has led to some softness in the stock after significant outperformance in the first half of 2023. This included the unfortunate resignation of COO Frederic-Mercier Langevin which followed the departure of its SVP Exploration & Resources Michael Michaud in July. The former was a significant loss for the company with Frederic-Mercier Langevin having 14 years of experience at Agnico Eagle (AEM) operating multiple mines (Lapa, Goldex, LaRonde) in the region, as well as time spent at one of its largest mines, Meliadine. Wesdome noted that he stepped down for personal reasons, but this is the second COO replacement in less than three years.

As for new appointments to fill these vacant roles, Wesdome recently announced that Guy Belleau would take over the COO role. He was previously spending time as CEO of Sayona Canada (subsidiary of Sayona Mining), as well as COO of ArcelorMittal (MT) and before that, in Mine Manager positions at Horne 5, Eleonore and Perserverance. Meanwhile, Kevin Lonergan has come in as Senior VP Technical Services, with his former role including Director of Mining and VP Mining at Ero Copper (ERO) with Wesdome's new CEO Anthea Bath. Finally, Wesdome has recruited Jono Lawrence who has a strong track record of adding ounces in Africa, including time spent at the massive former Randgold mine, Kibali, in the DRC.

Overall, these appointments have taken some uncertainty off the table and have Wesdome well-equipped on its plan to grow production to 200,000+ ounces after what should be a ~175,000 ounce year in 2024.

Valuation

Based on ~152 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$9.15, Wesdome continues to trade at a significant premium to its peer group of junior producers (sub 250,000-ounce producers) at a market cap of ~$1.39 billion. In fact, the company currently trades at a valuation of ~$950/oz of total M&I resources and is now back to trading at a premium to its estimated net asset value of ~$1.36 billion. Meanwhile, Wesdome now trades near ~12x FY2025 EV/FCF estimates ($2,300/oz gold price assumption) despite its recent pullback, also a significant premium to some of its larger producer peers.

It's important to note that some premium can easily be justified for its position as a Tier-1 jurisdiction producer with high-grade mines and a declining cost profile, with FY2025 AISC likely to move back below US$1,200/oz. That said, using what I believe to be fair multiples of 8.0x cash flow and 1.2x P/NAV for its premium status as a high-grade Tier-1 producer, I see a fair value for the stock of US$11.20. This points to a 22% upside from current levels which, while attractive to some, is not nearly enough margin of safety for me based on my requirement of a minimum 30% discount to fair value for Tier-1 only jurisdiction junior producers.

Hence, I would need a deeper pullback or low-risk technical setup to become interested in the stock from a swing-trading standpoint.

But what if $2,300/oz gold and $2,400/oz gold in 2025 and 2026 end up being too conservative?

If we take a look at how producers and Wesdome specifically look at $2,500/oz gold and $32.00/oz price assumptions in 2025 and 2026 from a cash flow standpoint, valuations improve materially, with Wesdome's fair value jumping to US$11.80. This still does not provide an adequate margin of safety per my entry rules, and while Wesdome gets more attractive if we assume higher metals prices, so do all other names in the sector. Therefore, higher gold prices do not make Wesdome any more attractive than its peers. In fact, that makes some of its peers in periods of heavier growth capex much more attractive, as it materially improves their free cash flow yields.

To summarize, while there continue to be a handful of extremely attractively names in the sector today that trade at massive discounts to fair value that could easily double or triple if they execute successfully, I don't see enough of a margin of safety in Wesdome today.

Summary

Wesdome Gold Mines Ltd. had another solid quarter in Q2-24 but after a ~100% rally off its lows, I see far more attractive bets elsewhere in the sector. From a bigger picture standpoint, Wesdome should be able to produce ~210,000 ounces at sub $1,150/oz AISC in 2026 and if the gold price holds up, it could easily enjoy a further re-rating to US$12.00 — US$13.00. However, with a weaker pipeline than peers and a far less attractive relative value proposition vs. some of its producer peers, I continue to focus elsewhere in the sector. That said, if WDOFF stock were to pull back closer to US$7.90, I would consider starting a new position in the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Yields Up To 9%: 3 REITs To Buy For 2025
data.description:
Over the past two years, fixed income investments were very popular among retail investors.

You could earn a 5% yield by simply putting capital in a money-market fund or buying short-term Treasuries, so why would run the risk of buying volatile stocks like REITs (VNQ)?

But times are changing rapidly.

The Fed already cut interest rates by 50 basis points, and it is widely expected to cut it by another 25 basis points in less than a month from now. The debt market is now pricing a 90% chance of that happening:

And that's not all.

The debt market predicts an additional ~150 basis point worth of cuts in 2025.

This means that interest rates are expected to drop from 5% to just around 3% within a year from now, making fixed income investments far less desirable.

However, REITs are still discounted and offering very attractive yields that are not only safe, but also growing. There are a number of them that offer dividend yields in the 7-9% range, but as interest rates are cut, I expect them to become increasingly popular and rise in value.

Here are three high yielding REITs that I expect to surge higher in 2025:

Easterly Government Properties (DEA): 8% Dividend Yield

DEA is a net lease REIT just like Realty Income (O), VICI Properties (VICI), and Agree Realty (ADC).

However, instead of buying retail-based net lease properties, it focuses primarily on properties that are leased to government agencies. This includes things like VA outpatient facilities and FBI headquarters.

As such, its rental income is backed by the full faith of the US government. What's even better is that it has 10+ years left on its leases on average, resulting in a highly consistent and predictable income.

Even then, it is today priced at an 8% dividend yield, which is 2x higher than the yield of the 10-year Treasury, and you get the added benefits of inflation protection since you own the real estate.

Why is it so cheap then?

I think that the company is misunderstood. I know this because I myself suffered significant misconceptions about the company until recently.

But I had the chance to interview the CEO of their company for High Yield Landlord, and he made a strong case as to why DEA is today undervalued.

Firstly, the market seems to think that the REIT has significant exposure to the office sector, but in reality, this exposure is "minimal" according to their CEO. The vast majority of its assets are highly specialized and do not compete with traditional office buildings:

Secondly, the market seems to worry about the company's leverage, which is a bit higher than that of its net lease peers like Realty Income (O), but their CEO explains to us that they are able to operate with a bit more leverage because they do not have any tenant credit risk. Companies like Realty Income need to account for credit risk and therefore, they also have to be more conservative with their debt. Moreover, their debt maturities are also well-staggered:

Thirdly, and perhaps most importantly, the market worries that DEA may need to cut their dividend, but their CEO makes it clear that they do not expect to cut it. Yes, the payout ratio is high at slightly above 100%, but they are confident that they can grow into the dividend and fully cover it already in the near term. They are able to acquire properties at a positive spread, and these spreads should only get larger as interest rates are cut. Moreover, They also expect to enjoy strong releasing spreads as their leases expire, and they bump up rents because the replacement cost of their assets has grown significantly in recent years.

I wasn't interested in DEA when it traded at a premium to its net lease peers, but following its recent underperformance, it has now become one of the cheapest in its peer group:

FFO Multiple
Easterly Government Properties (DEA) 11.4x
W. P. Carey (WPC) 12.7x
NNN REIT (NNN) 14.9x
Realty Income (O) 15.5x
Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) 17.3x
Agree Realty (ADC) 18.3x

I think that as DEA keeps proving the market wrong and eventually fully covers its dividend, the market will reward it with a 20-30% higher share price. Right now, you still have the chance to lock in its 8% dividend yield.

NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP): 9% Dividend Yield

I have already covered NLCP, but I will again because this is one of our largest holdings today.

We have invested heavily in it because we think that NLCP is one of just a few REITs that have the potential to deliver both high yield and high growth, at the same time.

This is exceptional because if a REIT is offered at a high yield, it is typically because it has poor growth prospects.

But this isn't the case here.

NLCP is offering a 9% dividend yield, but it should also be able to grow its cash flow and dividend by 5-10% annually going forward.

Where is this growth going to come from?

Firstly, the REIT enjoys near 3% annual rent escalators and its tenants are responsible for all property expenses, including even the maintenance, and therefore, this rent growth should fall straight to the bottom line.

Secondly, the REIT retains about 20% of its cash flow to reinvest in growth. So far, it has mostly used this cash flow to buy back shares, but now that its share price has recovered a bit, it will likely turn to property acquisitions.

Thirdly, the REIT today has no leverage, which means that it could expand its portfolio size by about 20-30% as it adds some debt to its balance sheet. This would be highly accretive to its FFO per share, since the cap rates on its new acquisitions easily exceed the interest rates that it would need to pay on this debt. The cap rates for cannabis cultivation facilities are in excess of 12%, but its interest rates would likely be around 6%.

That's a massive spread! Far bigger than what other net lease REITs like Realty Income are able to achieve.

And if you don't believe me, just look at the company's track record. It has grown rapidly each year ever since it went public and has managed to hike its dividend in nearly every quarter:

The combination of high yield and high growth should result in strong returns on its own, but I also expect additional upside from multiple expansion as interest rates are cut to lower levels. Simply repricing at a reasonable 14x FFO, in-line with its close peer Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), would result in 40% upside from here.

Modiv Industrial (MDV): 7% Dividend Yield

MDV is a simple story.

This used to be a diversified net lease REIT with significant exposure to office properties, but also industrial and retail assets.

The REIT market typically punishes such REITs that lack focus with a lower valuation, resulting also in a higher dividend yield.

But MDV took note of that and transformed itself into a more specialized REIT with a focus on manufacturing net lease properties. It sold most of its other assets and completely rebranded itself with a new name, logo, and website.

But the market appears to have missed this transformation because it is still very recent.

Just like NLCP, we expect MDV to enjoy attractive growth prospects over the long run because it enjoys attractive 2-3% annual rent escalators, and it is able to earn positive spreads over its cost of capital.

The 7% yield combined with some growth and multiple expansion could result in 15-20% annual total returns in the coming years, and this is why we own it as part of our Core Portfolio.

data.title: XLY: Consumer Discretionary Dashboard For October
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics in the consumer discretionary sector. It is also a top-down analysis of sector ETFs like Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLY), Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (FDIS) and Vanguard Consumer Discretionary ETF (VCR), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for retailing in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in retail companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance. A floor of -100 is set for VS and QS when the calculation goes below this value. It may happen when metrics in a subsector are very bad.

data.title: I Believe Gold Has A Bull Case In Q4
data.description:
Main Thesis / Background

The purpose of this article is to discuss the border market backdrop with a specific focus on gold and how it may continue to do well as we enter the home stretch of 2024. This is an asset class (precious metals) I have owned for the long-term, and this review is a follow-up to an article I wrote roughly six months ago when I suggested gold a bullish backdrop. Since that time, we can see that gold has indeed performed well. It has delivered double-digit returns that actually match the equity market:

As you can see, adding precious metal exposure to my portfolio has been a rewarding play in 2024. But, perhaps more importantly, I believe it will continue to be rewarding as we enter the new year as well. There are multiple reasons for this thesis, and I will take each in turn in this review.

Why The Strength In 2024?

For regular followers of the market, gold's rise this calendar year should not be a surprise. We constantly see headlines on the metal's performance, especially when it hits fresh highs - which has happened quite a few times recently:

Given this reality, I think it makes sense to examine the reasons behind this impressive run. After all, understanding the "why" is critical when conducting an evaluation on whether or not the performance (whether positive or negative) will continue.

So, what are the reasons gold has performed so well? If I had to list the top three, these would be it:

1. Central bank purchases

A trend that has been ongoing for the past year and a half has been aggressive central bank buying of gold (and some other precious metals). While central banks have long held gold in their reserves, the levels of buying have steadily increased and that has helped push the price per ounce higher. As the following graphic shows us, the level of reserves is quite significant at a number of central banks:

This helps to provide a steady stream of demand for the metal and also reinforces the legitimacy of owning this - whether for businesses or individuals. After all - if it is good enough for global central banks, why can't it be good enough for us as retail investors?

Looking ahead, I don't see anything on the horizon that is going to drastically change this dynamic. If anything, demand will probably continue at similar rates because there has been an uptick in financial volatility recently and the US debt level keeps on rising - often a catalyst for investors to view gold as a safe haven. As we push into Q4, both of these trends remain in place, and that suggests gold's rise has plenty of room to go.

2. Fed rate cuts

The second big theme behind gold's ride higher has been the decline of interest rates - not just in the US but around the world as well. While rate cuts have only started recently, the prospect of lower rates has been around for over a year. This has led investors into the metals space, with gold being the primary beneficiary. As one of the most time-tested of hedges for a declining USD and lower rate environment, it is no wonder that 2024 has been a great year for gold.

This is another trend that is not going away. While rates are indeed lower and that has justified gold's move higher, the fact is, this story is not done. The expectation is the Fed (and other central banks) are going to continue on their rate cutting path into 2025. In fact, futures data suggests the Fed will cut again at their next meeting in early November:

What I take away from this is that the macro-environment for gold through the prospect of lower interest rates is a theme that is as pertinent today as it was in January (and for most of 2024). This tailwind is going to keep gold's price elevated and suggests to me that the bull cycle for gold is not as late as it may appear on the surface. This supports my continued buy case.

3. Geopolitical Events (And Future Risks)

The third big mover for gold, in my view, has been the onslaught of geopolitical risks that have surfaced over the past couple of years. These involve the war between Russia and Ukraine, the escalation of the current conflict in the Middle East / Gaza, an upcoming US presidential election, a change in party leadership in the UK, and multiple others. None of these in isolation are enough to really move markets, but combined they represent significant challenges for international companies and heightened the risk of volatility in both equity and bond markets going forward.

The net result of this is that it makes safe haven assets more attractive in relative terms than when the world is at "peace". While there is always some global headline on the horizon, the current level of conflicts and uncertainty present real challenges that I, personally, believe the equity market is discounting (think all-time highs). In this type of environment, I see the legitimacy of gold as important as ever. And, similar to the other two topics discussed above, these are not geopolitical events that are going to go away overnight. The conflicts in both Ukraine and Gaza have been going on for a long time and don't seem ready to end. Similarly, if we see a second Trump presidency, I think there will be significant uncertainty as to what that will mean to markets because it is likely to be vastly different from the current administration.

Does this make me a "bear"? Of course not. But it does amplify why I see merit to owning equity hedges right now - and gold is one of the most famous equity hedges out there.

Retail Buying Has Been Relatively Small

As my followers know, I try to have a contrarian tilt when possible. This includes pretty much every stock/sector/fund I cover - including gold. So this may seem like an inopportune time to be adding to existing positions given gold's run and the hype surrounding it.

While I would not necessarily object to those concerns - because everyone needs to be comfortable with their own exposure and risk tolerance - I would point out that gold hasn't really been gaining that much favor with the retail crowd. Yes, buying has picked up steadily in 2024, but it is still relatively low compared to prior cycles. Further, this is coming on the backdrop of two years of strong ETF outflows from the retail base:

I have two primary conclusions from this graphic. One, it is clear that investors have been showing interest in gold this year. While I see the demand as somewhat muted, it is a far cry from the steady string of outflows that was the case for the past two years. So this is not a "pure" contrarian play that I would prefer.

Two, expanding on the latter point above, the return of demand has not been as robust relative to the pouring of outflows before the recent turn. With such consistent outflows for such an extended period, I would have expected inflows to come roaring back - and that hasn't really happened. This tells me that the tide has only partially shifted, and there is a good chance that stronger buying could be ahead. As a result, I actually think buying in now front-runs some eventual retail demand, despite gold sitting at record levels already.

Other Hedges Look Pricey Too

I have outlined my reasoning for why I see gold moving higher in the weeks and months to come. This has to do with fundamental factors, and it also has merit as an investment because of its usefulness of protecting portfolios during equity swoons. With the S&P 500 sitting near or at fresh highs almost daily, allocating some new cash to hedging positions makes a lot of sense to me.

Of course, gold isn't the only way to hedge equity risk. It is one of many, with quality bonds being the most common among both institutional and retail investors. This isn't a one or the other decision - an investor can certainly hold gold and bonds in their portfolio (as well as equities!).

Nevertheless, whenever we choose to buy one asset, it comes at the expense of all others. We never buy anything in isolation, there are always choices, and if one is concerned about equity valuations (as I am) then considering IG-rated fixed-income issues would be a natural place to start.

This is important because it actually helps emphasize why I like gold at this juncture. While bonds have been doing very well of late, they have perhaps been doing too well. What I mean by that is IG-rated corporate bonds have seen their spreads narrow to levels not seen in years:

This suggests that quality issues are getting quite pricey at the moment. While there is still a bull case that can be made, those who have waited until now to buy IG-rated debt are clearly a bit behind the curve. Unlike gold, I don't see as strong an investment case for corporate bonds - although they will undoubtedly push higher if the Fed proceeds with more rate cuts. But will the returns offered in this sector beat out gold? With spreads where they are, my thought is no. This helps justify why I like gold as a hedge in relative terms over some other options.

Bottom-line

Gold has been running higher, and I believe more gains are yet to come. The factors that have supported the recent move - elevated geopolitical risks, lower interest rates, and central bank demand - are all still in place as we move into Q4. Without a fundamental change to the macro-backdrop, I don't see a compelling reason to shift away from gold for the time being.

Is gold getting a bit expensive? Sure, I won't argue against that! But what quality asset isn't expensive at this moment? What is important is the move from gold has been well-supported, and the chances of the metal remaining in demand seem high to me. As a result, I will continue to build on to my existing position, and I suggest my followers give the idea some thought at this time.

**For gold exposure, I own the iShares Gold Trust (IAU). I have also recommended and previously owned the Sprott Gold and Silver Trust (CEF).

data.title: Eyeing Dividend Growers With Unusual Earnings Dates
data.description:
The bar is raised for Q3. With a handful of earnings reports delivered from major banks, companies from other sectors begin now to report results to the street. This quarter, though, the S&P 500’s EPS growth rate is not all that impressive, at under 5%.1 We discussed it last week in our Q4 2024 Investor Conference & Events Highlights missive, but there are new developments.

The US Dollar Index has been on the rise, interest rates continue to firm following the Fed’s first rate cut a month ago, and the threat of higher oil prices amid renewed geopolitical tensions is a worry. These are key potential macro headwinds, and while they may not be fully felt in earnings updates from multinationals in the weeks ahead, Q4’s stout bottom line growth rate could be called into question (not to mention 2025’s lofty S&P 500 EPS estimate).

Those in the C-suite don’t seem too worried. Dividend increase announcements are on the rise, according to Wall Street Horizon data, while corporations continue to be big buyers of their own stock.2 Throw in upper-end consumers who don’t seem to quit spending and the potential for improved economic activity from China following a bazooka stimulus package announced earlier this month, and the bull train has pressed on.

There are certainly risks out there, but take a step back in time to a year ago. Global stocks were in the throes of an old-fashioned 10% market correction, led lower by mega-cap tech. Worries about a potential war in the Middle East, lasting inflation, and a protracted period of high interest rates (remember “higher for longer”?) had the bears feeling in command as the fourth quarter began. The bulls eventually took charge, though, and the S&P 500 is up by more than 35%, dividends included, from 12 months ago. In fact, the bull market itself just blew out the candles on its second birthday last weekend.

So, will the momentum continue through year-end? The upside freight train is chugging hard - the SPX posting its best year-to-date return through Q3 since 1997.3 We’re on a bit of a dividend kick here at Wall Street Horizon, so let’s double-click on three companies that recently announced payout hikes - each has an upcoming earnings date that deviates from historical norms. Perhaps investors can glean clues as to where the market’s tracks will lead in the weeks ahead.

Academic research shows that when a company confirms a quarterly earnings date that is later than when they have historically reported, it’s typically a sign that the company will share bad news on their upcoming call, while moving a release date earlier suggests the opposite.4

Regions Financial (RF)

Regions reports its Q3 results on Friday, October 18 BMO (confirmed). A conference call follows the announcement. Three months ago, the Alabama-based Regional Banks industry company reported net income available to common shareholders of $477 million and earnings per diluted share of $0.52, which modestly topped estimates, according to Option Research & Technology Services (ORATS).5

CEO John Turner noted, “The company exceeded all minimum capital levels and maintained a preliminary stress capital buffer at the 2.5% floor in the recent Federal Reserve Supervisory Stress Test.”6 The Q2 EPS beat, and confirmation of successful stress test results, may have laid the foundation for a dividend increase.

Regions’ Board of Directors declared a quarterly common dividend of $0.25 per share, a 4% jump over the Q2 dividend amount. The increase built on a 20% increase last year, making it three consecutive years of dividend growth. For the upcoming report, the bank’s reporting date is earlier than usual. Further out, it remains to be seen how the yield curve’s de-inversion will impact the bank’s net interest income.

A.O. Smith (AOS)

Another US cyclical, A.O. Smith is an early-season reporter. You might not be familiar with this one, but dividend growth investors sure know about it. AOS is a holding in the First Trust SMID Cap Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (SDVY).7 The latest distribution hike came just last week. A 6% increase, and CEO Kevin Wheeler remarked, "We are proud to say the five-year compound annual growth rate of our dividend rate is approximately 8%, and that we have increased our dividend each year for over 30 years."8

Shares of the Dividend Aristocrat have underperformed the S&P 500 so far in 2024, though the stock is up close to 30% year-on-year. The uptrend’s pause is at least partly explained by three consecutive poor earnings reactions. Going back to January, AOS has declined by 2%, 5%, and 9% post-earnings in the last three events, respectively, despite reporting EPS beats each time, per ORATS.9

On October 2, the $12.6 billion market cap company confirmed its Q3 report to occur on Tuesday, October 22, two days earlier than average.

Philip Morris International (PM)

Last on today’s list is a stock that has been on fire. Bears on Philip Morris International are indeed up in smoke considering that the 2024 return is 32% as of October 9. The rally began in the second quarter after three years of lackluster price action. Even after the jolt, PM’s yield is 4.6%, more than three times that of the S&P 500.

On September 12, PM’s Board of Directors announced an increase of the company’s regular quarterly dividend by 3.8% to an annualized rate of $5.40 per share.10 Philip Morris continues to evolve its portfolio as the traditional cigarette business wanes. A new smoke-free future is its focus.

Investors will hear important updates on that progression when PM issues its Q3 earnings report on Tuesday, October 22 BMO. The company’s earnings date is modestly later than usual, according to Wall Street Horizon data.

The Bottom Line

Global companies appear to have a shareholder bias as record-high profits pour in. Dividends and buybacks are stout, while returns across most global stock markets have been impressive in the past year. Be on the lookout for more dividend news over the next few weeks and if firms can meet those elevated expectations.

data.title: GDMN: A Strategic Blend Of Gold Futures And Gold Miners
data.description:
By Aneeka Gupta & Christopher Gannatti, CFA

WisdomTree created the WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund (GDMN) with the goal of offering one strategy to provide exposure to both the price of gold and the equity performance of gold miners. If one considers a hypothetical $100 investment in the Fund:

  • $90 is exposed to a basket of gold mining stocks.
  • $10 is exposed to a basket of U.S. Treasury securities to serve as collateral for gold futures contracts.
  • $90 is exposed to gold futures contracts.

So, for a $100 hypothetical investment, the notional exposure is actually $180, split evenly between the equity of gold miners and the futures contracts on the price of gold. The positions are “long,” meaning that if the prices are appreciating, positive returns are generated, whereas if prices are falling, negative returns are generated. As of this writing in 2024, both the mining equities basket and the price of gold are up. Of course, the leverage inherent in the strategy could serve to increase volatility.

WisdomTree has a large array of ETFs covering many different asset classes. At any moment, one can go to the WisdomTree suite of tools (PATH) and find out which of these ETFs are performing the best - or the worst.

Running this analysis as of October 14, 2024, we saw that GDMN was WisdomTree’s top-performing ETF.

The top 5 ETFs at WisdomTree over this period are:

  • GDMN, as mentioned.
  • The WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Equity Strategy Fund (GDE), which is structured similarly to GDMN, except that instead of the equity component being 90% exposed to gold miner equities it is exposed to a broad-based basket of 500 of the largest market capitalization U.S. equities.
  • The WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Fund (QGRW), which is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index. The strategy focuses on some of the largest U.S. equities with high earnings growth expectations, as well as high return on equity and return on assets fundamentals.
  • The WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ), which is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Index. The strategy focuses on export-oriented companies across the spectrum of Japan’s equity market, and weights the companies by their cash dividends, leading to a certain valuation-sensible and globally-oriented approach to Japan’s stock market. Notably, exposure to the fluctuations of the Japanese yen to U.S. dollar exchange rate is hedged, so any currency appreciation or depreciation does not have a direct impact on the returns.
  • The WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Fund (EPS), which is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Index. The strategy focuses on profitable companies in the U.S. in the large-cap size segment, and weighting companies by their profits bakes in a valuation sensibility to the exposure.

In the spirt of balance, here are the bottom 5 ETFs at WisdomTree over this period:

  • The WisdomTree Battery Value Chain and Innovation Fund (WBAT) is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Battery Value Chain and Innovation Index. A significant part of the battery value chain, at least in 2024, goes through China and China’s equities have been performance-challenged for some time, contributing to this strategy being the worst-performing WisdomTree ETF over the period.

  • The WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence and Innovation Fund (WTAI) is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence and Innovation Index. Artificial intelligence in a “post-Chat GPT1” world, so far, has seen more strong equity performance in larger, very profitable companies as opposed to smaller, more speculative companies.

Reflections of a Gold-Focused Investor

The remarkable thing about the investment landscape in 2024 regards the incredible degree of choice available. It is natural to imagine that some investors are thinking about gold. They can think about:

  • Exposure to the metal itself: If the thought is to hedge against true disaster scenarios, the preference is often actual exposure to the physical metal. Some might even have safes on their property. Others might be comfortable with investment strategies that are backed up by holdings of physical gold.
  • Exposure to gold miners: A significant criticism to investing directly in gold, the metal, is that there are no cash flows coming from it. Equities can have dividends. Fixed income can have interest payments. Gold miners have a clear relationship with gold, as it’s possible they make more money with a higher gold price, but since they are companies, they can choose to distribute dividends to their shareholders.
  • Exposure to gold (the metal) and gold miners in a single investment: Gold and gold miners are never completely disconnected, but it’s also clear that equities in gold miners can have a very different return profile than that of gold. Earlier in this piece, we brought forward the concept behind GDMN, which represents an exposure to both gold and gold miners in a single strategy. We think that anyone thinking about a gold allocation should understand the differences between gold, the metal, gold mining equities and GDMN from a risk and return perspective.

Connecting investment strategies across these concepts:

  • Gold: The SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) represents an exposure backed up by physical gold that is in a vault. The strategy is designed to essentially track the price of gold, measured in U.S. dollars, and provide that specific return stream. GLD is the largest such investment strategy by assets under management.
  • Gold Miners: The VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) represents an exposure to equities of gold miners. It is the largest such investment strategy by assets under management.
  • We previously detailed the structure of GDMN earlier in this piece.

Figure 3: Year-to-Date Returns

Specifying these strategies allows us to look at how they are performing so far in 2024 - or really any period for which live history is available. In 2024, gold’s price has appreciated significantly, at times eclipsing the $2,600 per ounce level. The return of gold miners has also been positive.

We noted previously that GDMN represents a long exposure to both equities of gold miners and the gold price, expressed in futures. For each hypothetical $100 investment, $90 is exposed to the miners and $90 is exposed to the gold futures, for a total of $180. A year like 2024 - so far - with both components positive, creates a nice tailwind for the strategy.

Figure 4: Year-to-Date Performance

Miners Riding on Gold’s Coattails

As gold has been on a terrific run, a combination of factors is turning the tide in favor of gold - a weaker U.S. dollar, falling bond yields and central bank purchases alongside rising geopolitical risks. Up until now, gold linked ETF purchases were quite lackluster. However, the latest monthly statistics show that gold ETFs recorded inflows for the fourth month in a row in August.2 All regions showed rising ETF holdings, with North America and Europe posting the highest inflows.

The Federal Reserve has begun the easing of monetary policy, starting with a 50-basis point rate cut on September 18, 2024. The anticipation of further interest rate cuts over the coming months is enabling gold’s price to escalate as it lowers the opportunity cost of holding gold.

Figure 5: Gold versus U.S. Interest Rates

Gold Miners Can Act as a Leveraged Play on Gold

We noted before that gold miners are important beneficiaries of rising gold prices. The gold price is the single most important fundamental driver of earnings and returns on capital. Historically, gold mining stocks tend to act as a leveraged play on the gold price. We are beginning to see this trend play out in 2024. Since the start of the year, gold mining stocks are up 26.9%, while gold is up 24.6%.3 Gold mining stocks have plenty of catching up to do, as their recent outperformance versus gold is still at its nascency.

If we try to think of the reasons that held back gold miners’ performance versus gold, inflation does spring to mind. The rampant inflation we have been accustomed to since the COVID pandemic has been a key driver of gold miners’ operational costs, thereby resulting in an erosion of profits. Energy was the key cost driver accounting for almost half of the all-in sustaining costs (AISC) of gold miners. The higher interest rate environment has also raised borrowing costs for gold miners. Added to that, the increased focus on environmental, social and governance (ESG) investing principles has made it harder for miners to obtain capital investment. Gold miners bring in added complications as well, as tighter regulations tend to raise miners’ costs as they often operate in parts of the world where standards of governance and transparency remain weak.

Figure 6: Gold Miners Taking the Lead from Gold’s Price Performance

Gold Miners’ Operational Performance Set to Improve

The average AISC for primary gold operations are projected to drop by 4% in 2024 to an average of $1,218/oz, marking the first decline since 2016.4 Increased averaged gold grades and higher recoveries are also expected to help the overall cost profile of gold miners in 2024. The significant advancement in productivity is poised to drive margin expansion in this highly capital-intensive industry. More importantly, amid gold’s sustained move higher, industry cost pressures have begun to ease, paving the way for higher margins in H2 2024.

Figure 7: AISC Buoyed by Rising Gold Prices

Conclusion: A Marriage of Gold & Gold Miners

We always learn by looking at the top- and bottom-performing ETFs at WisdomTree over different periods. With GDMN in the top spot, we wanted to address the fact that many may not even realize such an investment - with exposure to BOTH the price movements of gold AND gold miner equities in ONE solution - exists. We think that anyone considering an exposure to gold or to gold miner equities should include this strategy, GDMN, in the analysis to note how it compares and to see if it is a better fit with an overall investment thesis on the gold space.
